content:
# Numbers Chapter 22 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 174–176 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_022_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_022_page_002.jpg`, `NUM_022_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0129–0130

---

**1** And the Sons of Israel set out and camped on the plains of Moab, across the Jordan from Jericho.

**2** And Balak son of Zippor saw everything that Israel had done to the Amorites.

**3** And Moab was terrified of the people because they were so vast. Moab was sick with dread because of the Sons of Israel.

**4** And Moab said to the elders of Midian, "Now this horde will devour everything around us the way an ox licks up the grass of the field." And Balak son of Zippor was king of Moab at that time.

**5** And he sent messengers to Balaam son of Beor, at Pethor, which is by the River in the land of the sons of his people, to summon him, saying: "Look — a people has come out of Egypt. They have spread across the face of the land, and they have settled right across from me.

**6** So please come now and curse this people for me, because they are too powerful for me. Perhaps then I will be able to strike them and drive them out of the land. For I know that whoever you bless is truly blessed, and whoever you curse is truly cursed."

**7** And the elders of Moab and the elders of Midian set out with fees for divination in their hands. And they came to Balaam and relayed Balak's message to him.

**8** And he said to them, "Stay here tonight, and I will bring you back whatever answer YHWH gives me." So the officials of Moab stayed with Balaam.

**9** And God came to Balaam and said, "Who are these men with you?"

**10** And Balaam said to God, "Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab, has sent word to me:

**11** 'Look — this people that has come out of Egypt has spread across the face of the land. Now come and curse them for me. Perhaps I will be able to fight against them and drive them out.'"

**12** And God said to Balaam, "You must not go with them. You must not curse the people, because they are blessed."

**13** And Balaam rose in the morning and said to the officials of Balak, "Go back to your own land, because YHWH has refused to let me go with you."

**14** And the officials of Moab got up and went back to Balak and said, "Balaam refused to come with us."

**15** And Balak sent yet more officials — more numerous and more distinguished than the first.

**16** And they came to Balaam and said to him, "This is what Balak son of Zippor says: 'Please — let nothing stop you from coming to me,

**17** for I will surely, surely honor you richly, and whatever you tell me to do I will do. So please — come and curse this people for me.'"

**18** And Balaam answered and said to the servants of Balak, "Even if Balak were to give me his palace full of silver and gold, I could not go beyond the word of YHWH my God, to do anything small or great.

**19** But please, you too stay here tonight, and let me find out what more YHWH has to say to me."

**20** And God came to Balaam at night and said to him, "If the men have come to summon you, get up and go with them. But only do exactly what I tell you to do."

**21** And Balaam rose in the morning, saddled his donkey, and went with the officials of Moab.

**22** But God's anger blazed because he was going, and the angel of YHWH stationed himself in the road as an adversary against him. And Balaam was riding on his donkey, and his two servants were with him.

**23** And the donkey saw the angel of YHWH standing in the road with his drawn sword in his hand. And the donkey turned off the road and went into the field. And Balaam struck the donkey to turn her back onto the road.

**24** Then the angel of YHWH stood in a narrow path between the vineyards, with a wall on this side and a wall on that side.

**25** And the donkey saw the angel of YHWH and pressed herself against the wall, crushing Balaam's foot against the wall. And he struck her again.

**26** And the angel of YHWH moved ahead once more and stood in a narrow place where there was no room to turn to the right or the left.

**27** And the donkey saw the angel of YHWH and lay down under Balaam. And Balaam's anger blazed, and he struck the donkey with his staff.

**28** Then YHWH opened the donkey's mouth, and she said to Balaam, "What have I done to you, that you have struck me these three times?"

**29** And Balaam said to the donkey, "Because you have made a fool of me! If I had a sword in my hand, I would kill you right now!"

**30** And the donkey said to Balaam, "Am I not your donkey, the one you have ridden all your life to this very day? Have I ever been in the habit of doing this to you?" And he said, "No."

**31** Then YHWH uncovered Balaam's eyes, and he saw the angel of YHWH standing in the road with his drawn sword in his hand. And he bowed his head and fell on his face.

**32** And the angel of YHWH said to him, "Why have you struck your donkey these three times? Look — I myself came out as an adversary, because your journey is reckless before me.

**33** And the donkey saw me and turned away from me these three times. If she had not turned away from me, by now I would surely have killed you and let her live."

**34** And Balaam said to the angel of YHWH, "I have sinned, for I did not realize that you were standing in the road to confront me. And now, if this is evil in your eyes, I will turn back."

**35** And the angel of YHWH said to Balaam, "Go with the men. But you may only speak the word that I speak to you." And Balaam went with the officials of Balak.

**36** And when Balak heard that Balaam had come, he went out to meet him at the city of Moab that is on the border of the Arnon — at the farthest edge of the territory.

**37** And Balak said to Balaam, "Did I not send urgently to you to summon you? Why did you not come to me? Am I really unable to honor you?"

**38** And Balaam said to Balak, "Look — I have come to you now. But do I have any power at all to say anything? The word that God puts in my mouth — that is what I must speak."

**39** And Balaam went with Balak, and they came to Kiriath-Huzoth.

**40** And Balak sacrificed cattle and sheep, and sent portions to Balaam and to the officials who were with him.

**41** And in the morning, Balak took Balaam and brought him up to Bamoth-Baal, and from there he could see the edge of the people.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "the plains of Moab" (ערבות מואב / 'arvot Mo'av):** The Hebrew *'arvot* denotes the arid steppe-land — the broad, flat plains east of the Jordan opposite Jericho. This is the staging ground for Israel's entry into Canaan and the setting for the entire Balaam narrative. The Leningrad Codex on page 174 places the beginning of chapter 22 in the left column, following the conclusion of chapter 21's victory accounts. The Vaticanus LXX (pa_0129, right column) reads similarly: *kai parenebalosin... epi tou Iordanou kata Ierichō*.

**Verse 3 — The double expression of dread (ויגר... ויקץ):** The Hebrew uses two distinct verbs: *wayyagor* (was terrified/in dread) and *wayyaqots* (was in anguish/loathing). The second verb *quts* carries a visceral, physical revulsion — a nausea-like dread. I rendered this pair as "terrified" and "sick with dread" to capture the escalation from fear to physical revulsion. The LXX uses *kai ephobēthē* (feared) and *kai prosōchthisen* (was distressed/vexed), confirming the two distinct emotional registers.

**Verse 4 — "the way an ox licks up the grass" (כלחך השור את ירק השדה):** This is one of the most vivid similes in the Torah. The verb *lakhakh* (to lick) appears here in a phrase that pictures a grazing ox methodically consuming every blade of green. The image is not of violent conquest but of relentless, total consumption — the land simply disappears. I preserved the simile directly because it is already dynamic and needs no softening. The elders of Moab speak to the elders of Midian, suggesting a military alliance against the common threat.

**Verse 5 — "Pethor, which is by the River" (פתורה אשר על הנהר):** Pethor is identified with Pitru on the Euphrates (Assyrian inscriptions confirm this). "The River" (*hannahar*) without qualification in Hebrew almost always means the Euphrates. The phrase "in the land of the sons of his people" (ארץ בני עמו / *'erets beney 'ammo*) is the Masoretic reading; the LXX and Samaritan Pentateuch read "the land of the sons of Ammon" (*'ammon*), a significant geographical variant. I follow the Leningrad Codex reading.

**Verse 6 — "whoever you bless is truly blessed, and whoever you curse is truly cursed":** The Hebrew here contains a powerful grammatical structure. The final clause uses the passive participles *mevorakh* (blessed) and *yu'ar* (cursed). I added "truly" to convey the emphatic certainty that Balak attributes to Balaam's oracular power. Balak believes Balaam's words have real, effective force — they are not mere wishes but performative speech acts.

**Verse 7 — "fees for divination" (קסמים בידם / qesamim beyadam):** The Hebrew *qesamim* could mean "divination instruments" or "divination fees." The context strongly suggests payment — these are diplomatic envoys carrying compensation for Balaam's professional services. The LXX reads *kai ta manteia en tais chersin autōn* (the divination-fees in their hands), supporting the interpretation of payment.

**Verse 8 — YHWH vs. Elohim:** A striking feature of this chapter is the alternation between the divine names. In v. 8, Balaam says "YHWH" (the covenant name), but in v. 9, the narrator says "God" (*Elohim*). This alternation is not random: when Balaam speaks to others, he uses YHWH (claiming intimate relationship); when the narrator describes God's actions toward a pagan diviner, *Elohim* (the universal name) appears. The Leningrad Codex preserves this pattern consistently.

**Verse 12 — "because they are blessed" (כי ברוך הוא):** God's reason for prohibiting the curse is not merely strategic but ontological — the people *are* blessed. The Hebrew *barukh hu'* is a statement of existing status, not future promise. This single phrase is the theological hinge of the entire Balaam cycle: no human curse can override a divine blessing.

**Verse 17 — "I will surely, surely honor you richly" (כבד אכבדך):** The Hebrew uses the infinitive absolute construction *kabbed 'akabbedekha* — literally "honoring I will honor you." Following the binding convention, this doubling is rendered with "surely, surely" to preserve the emphatic force of the infinitive absolute. Balak is offering unlimited wealth and prestige.

**Verse 18 — "his palace full of silver and gold" (מלא ביתו כסף וזהב):** The Hebrew *melo' beyto* means "the fullness of his house." In context, *bayit* refers to the royal palace — Balaam is saying that even the entire royal treasury would not be enough to make him defy YHWH. This is a grand rhetorical gesture, though as the narrative will show, Balaam's piety is more complicated than this speech suggests.

**Verse 22 — "God's anger blazed because he was going" (ויחר אף אלהים כי הולך הוא):** This verse creates a famous tension: God just told Balaam to go (v. 20), yet God is now angry that he went. The traditional interpretation is that God permitted the journey but was angry at Balaam's inner eagerness — he wanted to go and hoped to find a way to curse Israel for profit. The participle *holekh* (going) emphasizes the ongoing, willing nature of the action. The word *satan* (adversary) in this verse is not a proper name but a role — the angel positions himself as an obstacle, a blocker.

**Verse 23 — The donkey's three-fold refusal:** The narrative structure of vv. 23–27 follows a classic three-fold pattern: open road, narrow vineyard path, impossibly narrow passage. Each time the space shrinks, the donkey's options narrow, and Balaam's violence escalates. This is masterful storytelling — the animal sees what the prophet cannot. The irony is devastating: the professional seer is blind, while the dumb beast perceives the divine.

**Verse 28 — "YHWH opened the donkey's mouth" (ויפתח יהוה את פי האתון):** The Hebrew is matter-of-fact — no magical incantation, no special formula. YHWH simply *opens* the mouth, and the donkey speaks. The verb *patakh* (to open) is the same verb used of God opening wombs (Gen 29:31) and opening eyes (v. 31 below). The passage treats the miracle with striking narrative restraint. The LXX (Vaticanus, pa_0130) renders this straightforwardly: *kai ēnoixen ho theos to stoma tēs onou*.

**Verse 29 — "you have made a fool of me" (כי התעללת בי):** The Hebrew *hit'allalt* is a strong verb meaning "to abuse, mock, deal wantonly with." It is the same root used of the plagues God inflicted on Egypt (Exod 10:2). Balaam uses the language of divine judgment against his own donkey — another layer of irony. I rendered it as "made a fool of me" to capture the humiliation Balaam feels.

**Verse 30 — "Have I ever been in the habit of doing this?" (ההסכן הסכנתי):** The Hebrew uses the infinitive absolute *hasken hiskanti* — "being in the habit, have I been in the habit?" The doubling (per binding convention) would be "habitually, habitually" but this is one case where the dynamic approach clarifies: the donkey's rhetorical question demands the answer "No" — and Balaam gives it. He is forced into dialogue with an animal, and the animal wins the argument. The LXX confirms with *ei hyperēphania hyperēphaneusa* (have I ever treated you insolently?), though with a slightly different nuance.

**Verse 31 — "YHWH uncovered Balaam's eyes" (ויגל יהוה את עיני בלעם):** The verb *galah* (to uncover, reveal) suggests that Balaam's blindness was a covering rather than an absence — the spiritual reality was always there; Balaam simply could not perceive it. This is theologically rich: divine revelation is not the creation of something new but the removal of a veil. The same verb is used of prophetic revelation throughout the Hebrew Bible.

**Verse 32 — "your journey is reckless before me" (כי ירט הדרך לנגדי):** The Hebrew *yarat* is extremely rare — it appears only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. Its meaning is debated: "reckless," "precipitate," "contrary," or "perverse." The LXX renders it with a phrase suggesting the journey was "not worthy" or "not seemly" before God. I chose "reckless" to convey both the moral recklessness and the headlong haste of Balaam's journey.

**Verse 33 — "I would surely have killed you and let her live":** Another devastating irony: the angel would have killed the prophet and spared the donkey. The one who sees is more worthy of life than the one who is blind. The Hebrew *gam 'otkhah haragti we'otah hekheyiti* places "you" and "her" in sharp contrast with the direct object marker *'et*, making the reversal grammatically emphatic.

**Verse 38 — "do I have any power at all to say anything?" (היכל אוכל דבר מאומה):** Balaam's question uses the infinitive absolute *yakol 'ukhal* — "being able, am I able?" This is not false modesty but genuine theological constraint: the diviner acknowledges that he is a vessel, not a source. Whatever God places in his mouth is what must come out. The entire Balaam cycle hinges on this principle — human speech is subordinate to divine speech.

**Verse 40 — "sent portions" (וישלח):** The Hebrew *wayyishslakh* here means "sent" in the sense of distributing sacrificial portions. The sacrifice at Kiriath-Huzoth is a diplomatic courtesy — Balak hosts Balaam with a feast. The LXX specifies the sending more explicitly. This verse transitions from travel narrative to ritual preparation for the oracles that will follow in chapters 23–24.

**Verse 41 — "Bamoth-Baal" (במות בעל):** Literally "the high places of Baal" — a Moabite cultic site from which Balaam could survey the Israelite camp below. The name is loaded with irony: Balak brings Balaam to a site dedicated to Baal, hoping for a curse, but the God who will speak through Balaam is YHWH. The phrase "the edge of the people" (*qetseh ha'am*) suggests Balaam could see only the outer fringe of the camp — not its full, terrifying extent. This partial view sets up the dramatic panorama of the blessing oracles to come.

**On the Leningrad Codex manuscript:** Pages 174–176 of the Leningrad Codex present Numbers 22 across three folio sides in the characteristic three-column format with Tiberian Masoretic vocalization. The *masorah parva* (marginal annotations) appears in the margins, and the *masorah magna* runs along the top and bottom margins. The text is clearly legible with standard scribal conventions. The parashah break between chapter 21 and chapter 22 is marked with an open section (*petuhah*) marker, consistent with the major narrative transition from Israel's military victories to the Balaam pericope.

**On the Vaticanus LXX (pa_0129–0130):** The Greek text of Numbers 22 spans the right column of pa_0129 and continues through pa_0130. The uncial script is continuous (no word separation), characteristic of the fourth-century codex. The Greek follows the Hebrew closely throughout this chapter, with the most notable feature being the consistent use of *Balaam* (Βαλααμ) and *Balak* (Βαλακ) in their Greek transliterations. The divine name appears as *Kyrios* (Lord) where the Hebrew has YHWH, following standard LXX convention.

— PNEUMA
